title: AASEG: ATTENTION AWARE NETWORK FOR REAL TIME SEMANTIC SEGMENTATION [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## AASEG: ATTENTION AWARE NETWORK FOR REAL TIME SEMANTIC SEGMENTATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

In this paper, we present a new network named Attention Aware Network (AASeg) for real time semantic image segmentation. Our network incorporates spatial and channel information using Spatial Attention (SA) and Channel Attention (CA) modules respectively. It also uses dense local multi-scale context information using Multi Scale Context (MSC) module. The feature maps are concatenated individually to produce the final segmentation map. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method using a comprehensive analysis, quantitative experimental results and ablation study using Cityscapes, ADE20K and Camvid datasets. Our network performs better than most previous architectures with a 74.4% Mean IOU on Cityscapes test dataset while running at 202.7 FPS.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Semantic Segmentation is a fundamental problem in computer vision where the goal is to label each and every pixel in the image to its appropriate class. Since it is required to be deployed in real world settings like robots and autonomous vehicles, hence there is a need to balance the speed vs performance tradeoff. Fully Convolutional Network (FCN) (Long et al., 2015) composed of convolutional layers was one of the first works to get strong semantic representation.

However, this method was not able to capture boundary information accurately. Atrous convolutions (Yu and Koltun, 2015) at the last several stages of their network was used to give feature maps with strong semantic representation, thus solving the problem with FCN based architectures. However, this comes at at the cost of increased computational complexity. For real world navigation tasks like autonomous driving, there is a need to improve the Frame Per Second(FPS).

Chen et al. (2017) and (Yu and Koltun, 2015) used dilated convolutions to increase the recpetive field while maintaining the number of parameters. SegNet (Badrinarayanan et al., 2017) utilizes a small network structure and the skip-connected method to achieve improved FPS. (Mehta et al., 2018), (Paszke et al., 2016) and (Poudel et al., 2019) proposed unique approaches to tackle real time semantic segmentation problem.

## 2 RELATED WORK

(Fu et al., 2019) introduces spatial-wise and channelwise attention modules to enhance the recpetive field (Paszke et al., 2016) trims a a lot of convolution filters to reduce computation. ICNet (Zhao et al., 2018a) proposed an image cascade network using multiresolution branches. (Iandola et al., 2016) allows the neural network to find the critical channels of the feature map and select the most suitable channels by itself.

ESPNet (Mehta et al., 2018) introduces an efficient spatial pyramid (ESP), which brings great improvement in both speed and performance. Bilateral Segmentation Network (BiSeNet) (Yu et al., 2018a) used two parts: Spatial Path (SP) is used to get with the loss of spatial information and Context Path (CP) for compressing the receptive field. (Yu et al., 2020) used multi-path framework to combine the low-level details and high-level semantics.

(Li et al., 2019b) utilizes a light-weight backbone to speed up its network and a multi scale feature aggregation to improve accuracy. SwiftNet (Orsic et al., 2019) used lateral connections to restore

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

the prediction resolution while maintaining the speed. (Lin et al., 2017) uses a multipath refinement network to refine the feature but ignores the global context feature.
The speed-Accuracy performance comparison of state of the art methods on the Cityscapes test set is shown in Figure 1:
Figure 1: Speed-Accuracy performance comparison on the Cityscapes test set. Our approach achieves higher mean IOU while still being faster than most existing methods.

.

We summarize our main contributions as follows:

• We propose a novel Attention Aware Network (AASeg) for real time semantic segmentation.

• Our network is comprised of three parts: Spatial Attention(SA) module to capture the spatial dependencies of the feature maps, Channel Attention(CA) module to extract high level semantic information and Multi Scale Context(MSC) module to learn the information flow between feature maps of consecutive levels.

• Detailed experiments and analysis indicate the efficacy of our proposed network in not only improving the performance but also FPS. We achieve results on par with previous state of the art using Cityscapes, Camvid and on ADE20K datasets.

## 3 BACKGROUND

## 3.1 SPATIAL INFORMATION

The spatial information of the image is important to predict the detailed output for semantic segmentation. Modern existing approaches uses encoding of spatial information. DUC (Wang et al., 2018), PSPNet (Zhao et al., 2017), DeepLab v2 (Chen et al., 2017) use the dilated convolution to preserve the spatial size of the feature map.

## 3.2 CONTEXT INFORMATION

Semantic segmentation requires context information to generate a high-quality result. Most of the used methods enlarge the receptive field or fuse different contextual information. (Chen et al., 2017), (Wang et al., 2018) and (Yu and Koltun, 2015) use different dilation rates in convolution layers to capture different scale contextual information. In (Chen et al., 2017), an “ASPP” module is used to capture context information of different receptive field. PSPNet (Zhao et al., 2017) applies a “PSP” module which contains several different scales of average pooling layers.

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## 3.3 ATTENTION MECHANISM

(Hu et al., 2018) applied channel attention for image recognition and achieve the state-of-the-art. (Yu et al., 2018b) proposed a network that learns the global context as attention and revise the features. Multi scale network along with a custom attention module was proposed by (Sagar and Soundrapandiyan, 2020) for semantic segmentation.

## 3.4 FEATURE FUSION

The spatial information captured by the Spatial Path consists of rich detailed information. The output feature of the Context Path is made up of contextual information. Feature fusion was used to achieve state of the art results on image classification, object detection and instance segmentation using DMSANet (Sagar, 2021b).

## 4 PROPOSED METHOD

## 4.1 DATASET

The following datasets have been used to benchmark our results:

**1. Cityscapes** It is used for urban street segmentation. The 5000 annotated images are used in our experiments which are divided into 2975, 500 and 1525 images for training, validation, and testing respectively.
**2. ADE20K** This dataset contains labels of 150 object categories. The dataset includes 20k,2k and 3k images for training, validation and testing respectively.
**3. CamVid** This dataset is used for semantic segmentation for autonomous driving scenarios. It is composed of 701 densely annotated images.

## 4.2 NETWORK ARCHITECTURE

The fundamental goal in semantic segmentation is to map an RGB image _X ∈ R[H][×][W][ ×]_[3] to a semantic map _Y ∈ R[H][×][W][ ×][C]_ with the same spatial resolution _H × W_ , where _C_ is the number of classes of objects present in image. The input image _X_ is converted to a set of feature maps _Fl_ where l=1,...,3 from each network stage, where _Fl ∈ R[H][l][W][l][C][l]_ is a _Cl_ -dimensional feature map.

Our network dosen’t use any backbone to extract features from the input image unlike many previous architectures. The input image is first passed through a block comprising of convolutional, batch normalization and ReLU activation function.

We express a convolution layer _W[n]_ ( _x_ ) as follows:

where _⊙_ represents the convolution operator, _W[n][×][n]_ represents the _n × n_ convolutional kernel, _x_ represents the input data and _b_ represents the bias vector.

## 4.3 MULTI SCALE CONTEXT MODULE

The feature map after passing through convolutional block is split to three different parts with 1 _×_ 1 convolution, 3 _×_ 3 convolution and 5 _×_ 5 convolution respectively. The individual feature maps are then fused together. The output fused feature map is first convolved with a 1 _×_ 1 convolution to reduce the number of channels from 2048 to 256. The feature map produced is of size _H × W × nc_ , where _H, W_ are the height and width of the feature map, and _nc_ denotes the number of channels.

The input feature map is convolved with dilated convolution layers with increasing dilation rates of 3, 6 and 12. The dilated convolution layer input at every stage is formed by concatenating the input feature map with the outputs from previous convolutions. At the final step, the outputs from the three dilated convolutions are concatenated with the input feature map.

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## 4.4 SPATIAL ATTENTION MODULE

The spatial attention module is used for capturing the spatial dependencies of the feature maps. The spatial attention (SA) module in our network is defined below:

where _W_ 1 and _W_ 2 denotes the first and second 1 _×_ 1 convolution layer respectively, _x_ denotes the input data, _fSigmoid_ denotes the sigmoid function, _fReLU_ denotes the ReLu activation function.

The spatial attention module used in this work is shown in Figure 2:

Figure 2: Details of our spatial attention module

.

## 4.5 CHANNEL ATTENTION MODULE

The channel attention module is used for extracting high level multi-scale semantic information. The channel attention (CA) module in our network is defined below:

where _W_ 1 and _W_ 2 denotes the first and second 1 _×_ 1 convolution layer, _x_ denotes the input data. _fAvgP ool_[1][denotes the global average pooling function,] _[ f][Sigmoid]_[ denotes the Sigmoid function,] _[ f][ReLU]_ denotes ReLU activation function.

The channel attention module used in this work is shown in Figure 3:

Figure 3: Details of our channel attention module

.

## 4.6 AGGREGATION

We denote the concatenation operation as follows:

where _⊕_ represents the concatenation operator and _x_ 1, _x_ 2 and _x_ 3 represents the features of the two branches. The AASeg module can be denoted as follow:
_xAASeg_ = (( _fSA_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ ) _⊕_ ( _fCA_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ ) _⊕_ ( _fMSC_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ )) (5)

where _⊕_ represents the concatenation operator, _fCA_ represents the channel attention module mentioned in Equation 2, _fSA_ represents the spatial attention module, _fMSC_ represents the multi scale attention and _xconcat_ represents the combined feature.
we use _ConvXi_ to denote the operations of _i[th]_ block. Therefore, the output of _i[th]_ block is calculated as follows:

where _ConvX_ includes one of each convolutional layer, batch normalization layer and ReLU activation layer, _ki_ is the kernel size of convolutional layer, _xi−_ 1 and _xi_ are the input and output of _i[th]_ block. Fusion operation is used to combine high-level features with low-level features using Equation 7:

The overall structure of our proposed AASeg network is shown in Figure 4.

Figure 4: Architecture of proposed AASeg network architecture. “c” denotes concatenation.

.

## 4.7 LOSS FUNCTIONS

We use cross-entropy loss function to weigh the difference between the forward propagation result of the network and the ground truth of the samples. The cross-entropy loss is calculated as defined in Equation 8:

where _N_ denotes the total number of samples, _yn_ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is true, and 1- _yn_ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is false.
We also use the auxiliary supervision loss _Laux_ to improve the model performance thus making it easier to optimize. The auxiliary loss can be defined as:

5

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

where _B_ is the mini batch size, _N_ is the number of pixels in every batch; _K_ is the number of categories; _pijk_ is the prediction of the _j[th]_ pixel in the _i[th]_ sample for the _k[th]_ class, _I_ ( _gij_ = _k_ ) is a function which is defined in Equation 10.
The class attention loss _Lcls_ from channel attention module is also used. The class attention loss is
where _aijk_ is the value generated of the class attention map of the _j[th]_ pixel in the _i[th]_ sample for the _k[th]_ class.

where _λ_ 1, _λ_ 2 and _λ_ 3 are set as 1, 0.5 and 0.5 to balance the loss.

## 4.8 IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS

PyTorch deep learning framework is used to carry out our experiments. Our neural networks is trained with stochastic gradient descent (SGD) as optimizer, batch size of 16, momentum of 0.9 and weight decay of 5 _e[−]_[4] . The network is trained for 20K iterations with an initial learning rate value of 0.01. The, “poly” learning rate policy is used to decay the initial learning rate while training the model to reduce over-fitting.
Data augmentation operations like random horizontal flip, random resizing with scale range of [1.0, 2.0], and random cropping with crop size of 1024 _×_ 1024 was done.

## 4.9 EVALUATION METRICS

For quantitative evaluation of the performance of our network, mean of class-wise intersection-overunion (mIoU) is used for bench-marking the performance, number of float-point operations (FLOPs) and frames per second (FPS) are used for bench-marking the speed.

## 5 RESULTS

We present the segmentation accuracy and inference speed of our proposed method on Cityscapes validation and test set in Table 1. We use the training set and validation set to train our models before submitting to Cityscapes online server.

The qualitative results on Cityscapes validation set are presented in Figure 5:

.

We evaluate our method on CamVid dataset. The comparison results with other methods is shown in Table 2:

The comparison of our network with previous state of the art methods for semantic segmentation using ADE20K validation set is shown in Table 3:
Table 3: Performance comparison with results reported on ADE20K validation set. Best results are highlighted in bold.

## 6 ABLATION STUDY

We explore the effect of 3 different upsamling operations: bilinear upsampling, deconvolution and nearest neighbor upsampling as shown in Table 4. The best results are obtained using bilinear upsampling but there isn’t considerable difference.
Table 4: Ablation study on Upsampling operation in our network using Cityscapes validation set. Best results are highlighted in bold.

|old.||
|---|---|
|Method|mIoU (%)|
|bilinear upsampling|**79.2**|
|deconvolution|78.5|
|nearest neighbor|78.3|
We also try the various kernel size in Table 5. Larger kernel size of 7 _×_ 7 is also tried which results in a similar performance but increases computational complexity.

We use increasing sequence of dilation rates in our network, which is 0, 1, 2, 3. It seems large dilation rates increases the performance but comes at the cost of increased number of parameters, hence decrease in FPS. This effect is shown in Table 6:

8

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Table 5: Ablation study on kernel size k in our network using Cityscapes validation set. Best results are highlighted in bold.
Table 6: Ablation study results on Cityscapes validation set. FPS are estimated for an input image of resolution of 512 _×_ 1024. Best results are highlighted in bold.

## 7 CONCLUSIONS

In this paper, we present a new network named Attention Aware Network (AASeg) for real time semantic segmentation. We use Spatial Attention(SA) and Channel Attention(CA) modules to enhance features of objects without adding extra computational cost. The fused spatial-channel attention along with Multi Scale Context(MSC) module enables network to extract discriminative and robust features of targets or background. We test our network using Cityscapes, ADE20K and Camvid datasets. Our network balances speed-performance tradeoff at par with previous state of the art network architectures. In the future, we would like to use multi scale attention module for instance segmentation problem.

## ACKNOWLEDGMENTS

We would like to thank Nvidia for providing the GPUs.

This document was extracted by OCR engine and could contain mistakes.

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents a real-time semantic segmentation network (AASeg) claiming state-of-the-art trade-offs between speed and accuracy. While the motivation is clear, the technical execution is marred by significant internal inconsistencies, likely exacerbated by OCR or drafting errors, that make the architecture and results difficult to verify. Key equations for attention modules (Eq 2 and 3) do not match their respective diagrams (Fig 2 and 3), and Eq 5 introduces a new term 'fMSC' (multi-scale attention) that is not formally defined or represented in the architecture diagram (Fig 4), which instead describes a 'Multi Scale Context' (MSC) module. Most critically, the ablation studies (Tables 4, 5, and 6) report mIoU scores around 79-80%, which contradict the main results in Table 1 (74.8%), suggesting either a massive discrepancy in experimental setup or potentially fabricated/misplaced data.

comments.title: Severe mismatch between main results and ablation studies
comments.quote: AASeg [Cityscapes val] 74.8 [Table 1] vs AASeg-baseline 79.2 [Table 6]
comments.explanation: In Table 1, the authors report a mIoU of 74.8% for the Cityscapes validation set. however, in the ablation studies (Tables 4, 5, and 6), every configuration (including the baseline) reports scores significantly higher, ranging from 77.4% to 80.2%. It is highly unusual for a baseline in an ablation study to outperform the 'final' model reported in the main comparison table by ~5%. This suggests that the ablation studies might have been conducted on a different dataset, a different split, or contain numerical errors that undermine the paper's quantitative reliability.

comments.title: Inconsistency between channel attention formula and diagram
comments.quote: fCA(x) = fSigmoid(W2(fReLU(W1(fAvgPool(x))))) [Equation 3]
comments.explanation: Equation 3 describes a standard Squeeze-and-Excitation style block. However, Figure 3 ('Details of our channel attention module') shows a much more complex structure involving Max Pooling and Average Pooling in parallel, followed by addition, which is more reminiscent of the BAM or CBAM modules. The text says one thing (Eq 3), but the figure shows another. This makes the implementation non-reproducible.

comments.title: Missing definition for fMSC in aggregation formula
comments.quote: xAASeg = ((fSA(xconcat) ⊗ xconcat) ⊕ (fCA(xconcat) ⊗ xconcat) ⊕ (fMSC(xconcat) ⊗ xconcat)) [Equation 5]
comments.explanation: Equation 5 introduces a function 'fMSC' and refers to it as 'multi scale attention'. However, Section 4.3 describes a 'Multi Scale Context' (MSC) module which is a sequence of dilated convolutions, not an attention mechanism designed to be multiplied (⊗) by the concatenated features. Furthermore, Fig 4 shows the MSC leading into the concatenation, not operating on the results of the concatenation. This creates a logical loop: xconcat is formed by x1, x2, x3, but the equation says these functions operate on xconcat.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in spatial attention formula
comments.quote: fSA(x) = fSigmoid(W2(fReLU(W1(x)))) [Equation 2]
comments.explanation: Spatial attention typically involves reducing the channel dimension to 1 (often via pooling or 1x1 conv) to create a spatial mask. The formula in Eq 2 describes two 1x1 convolutions with a ReLU. If x is [H, W, C], and W1/W2 are 1x1 convolutions, the output remains a high-dimensional feature map unless W2 reduces the filter count to 1. Figure 2, however, shows a 'Spatial Pool' and 'Max Pool' followed by a 'Conv 7x7', which contradicts the formula (which specifies 1x1 convolutions W1 and W2).

comments.title: Logical circularity in aggregation and fusion description
comments.quote: xconcat = x1 ⊕ x2 ⊕ x3 [Equation 4] ... xAASeg = ( ... fSA(xconcat) ... ) [Equation 5]
comments.explanation: The text in Section 4.2 says the image is converted to feature maps Fl (l=1...3). Figure 4 shows the image entering a block, then splitting into three paths that eventually concatenate. However, Equation 5 suggests the attention modules (SA, CA) take the 'concatenated' feature as input. If the attention modules are what create the paths that are eventually concatenated (as per Fig 4), they cannot take the final concatenation as an input without a recurrency that is not described.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'no backbone' claim
comments.quote: Our network dosen’t use any backbone to extract features from the input image unlike many previous architectures.
comments.explanation: The authors claim no backbone is used, yet they report 2048 channels in Section 4.3 ('reduce the number of channels from 2048 to 256'). Achieving 2048 channels from a single 'convolutional block' as described in 4.2 is computationally expensive and atypical for real-time models. It's unclear if 'no backbone' means they designed a custom encoder (which is still a backbone) or if the architecture in Fig 4 is the entirety of the network. If the latter, the channel counts and depth are underspecified.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel real-time semantic segmentation network, AASeg, that integrates spatial, channel, and multi-scale context attention mechanisms. The architecture is well-motivated, and the experimental evaluation is comprehensive across multiple datasets. The results show competitive performance in both mIoU and FPS, especially compared to backbone-free models. However, several technical issues in the formulation of the attention modules, loss functions, and network aggregation raise concerns about reproducibility and correctness. While the overall claims are plausible, these issues—particularly in the mathematical formulation—undermine confidence in the precise implementation and reported gains.

comments.title: Incorrect cross-entropy loss formulation
comments.quote: where _N_ denotes the total number of samples, _yn_ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is true, and 1- _yn_ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is false.
comments.explanation: The cross-entropy loss as described appears to be formulated for binary classification, using a single probability _yn_ and its complement. However, semantic segmentation is a multi-class pixel-wise classification task, which requires a multi-class cross-entropy loss defined over K classes and N pixels. The given formulation does not account for per-pixel class labels or softmax/log-softmax outputs across multiple classes. While the later auxiliary loss (Equation 9) correctly indexes over pixels and classes, the primary loss in Equation 8 is fundamentally mismatched to the task. This is not a mere simplification—it misrepresents the core training objective. Even assuming _yn_ refers to the correct class probability, the notation and lack of class indexing make this highly misleading.

comments.title: Inconsistent concatenation in AASeg module definition
comments.quote: _xAASeg_ = (( _fSA_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ ) _⊕_ ( _fCA_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ ) _⊕_ ( _fMSC_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ )) (5)
comments.explanation: The equation uses _⊕_ to denote concatenation (as defined earlier), and _⊗_ which is not formally defined but appears to denote element-wise multiplication based on attention module conventions. However, the structure implies concatenation of three modulated feature maps: spatial-attended, channel-attended, and MSC-attended features. This is problematic because _fSA(xconcat) ⊗ xconcat_ and _fCA(xconcat) ⊗ xconcat_ are both the same size as _xconcat_, so concatenating them would result in a 3× channel output. But this contradicts standard attention usage, where attention is applied additively or multiplicatively, not concatenated. The authors likely intend to combine the three attention outputs before applying to _xconcat_, or to sum them. As written, it suggests a parallel application and concatenation of three full feature maps, which is not typical and would drastically increase channel dimensions without any mention of subsequent dimensionality reduction. This makes the architecture unreproducible as stated.

comments.title: Missing definition of _⊗_ operator
comments.quote: _fSA_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_
comments.explanation: The symbol _⊗_ is used in Equation 5 to combine attention maps with feature maps, likely intended to denote element-wise multiplication, which is standard in attention mechanisms (e.g., SE-Net). However, the paper never defines this operator. While _⊙_ is defined as convolution, _⊗_ appears without any explanation. Given that _⊕_ is defined as concatenation, the lack of definition for _⊗_ creates ambiguity. Although experts might infer it as Hadamard product, this is a notation gap that affects reproducibility, especially for readers less familiar with attention conventions.

comments.title: Incoherent class attention loss definition
comments.quote:
The class attention loss is 

where _aijk_ is the value generated of the class attention map of the _j[th]_ pixel in the _i[th]_ sample for the _k[th]_ class.
comments.explanation: The class attention loss _Lcls_ is introduced without a formula. The text ends at 'is' and cuts off. This is a critical omission: the loss is claimed to come from the channel attention module, but without its mathematical form, it is impossible to understand or reproduce the training process. While it might be intended as a regularizer (e.g., promoting sparsity or consistency in attention maps), the absence of any equation or further description renders this component entirely underspecified. Given that it is weighted in the total loss with _λ_3 = 0.5, its omission is a major flaw.

comments.title: Misleading auxiliary loss indexing and undefined terms
comments.quote: where _B_ is the mini batch size, _N_ is the number of pixels in every batch; _K_ is the number of categories; _pijk_ is the prediction of the _j[th]_ pixel in the _i[th]_ sample for the _k[th]_ class, _I_ ( _gij_ = _k_ ) is a function which is defined in Equation 10.
comments.explanation: The auxiliary loss (Equation 9) uses indices _i_, _j_, _k_, but then references an indicator function _I(gij = k)_, which is not shown (Equation 10 is missing). Standard practice would define this as 1 if the ground truth label of pixel j in sample i is k, else 0. While this is inferable, its absence introduces risk of misinterpretation. More importantly, the loss structure appears to sum over _i_, _j_, _k_—but in pixel-wise cross-entropy, one typically sums over pixels and classes with the true label. The current form risks double-counting or incorrect normalization unless the indicator function ensures only the correct class contributes. Without Equation 10, this remains ambiguous. Additionally, _N_ is defined as 'number of pixels in every batch', which is H×W×B, but this is not used in the equation—suggesting possible confusion in variable use.

comments.title: Inconsistent mIoU values between ablation and main results
comments.quote:
Table 4: Ablation study on Upsampling operation... bilinear upsampling|**79.2**
Table 1: AASeg|mIoU test(%)|74.4
comments.explanation: The ablation study (Table 4) reports a mIoU of 79.2% on the Cityscapes validation set for the best upsampling method, while the main result in Table 1 reports only 74.8% mIoU (val) for AASeg. This large discrepancy (over 4 points) cannot be explained by test vs val split or resolution differences alone. The ablation appears to report a higher-performance variant (possibly with larger kernels or dilation) than the final model. This raises concerns about consistency: either the ablation uses a different model configuration not described, or the 79.2% is a typo. Given that ablation studies should reflect variants of the final model, such a gap suggests a lack of alignment between experimental sections.

comments.title: Underspecified Multi-Scale Context module
comments.quote: The feature map after passing through convolutional block is split to three different parts with 1 × 1 convolution, 3 × 3 convolution and 5 × 5 convolution respectively. The individual feature maps are then fused together.
comments.explanation: The description of the MSC module lacks critical details: Are the convolutions applied in parallel or sequentially? How are the outputs fused—concatenation, summation, or another method? The text says the output is convolved with 1×1 to reduce channels from 2048 to 256, but no prior mention justifies the 2048-channel input. Furthermore, the subsequent description about dilated convolutions with rates 3,6,12 and concatenation is ambiguous: Is this an alternative or additional to the first path? The lack of clarity in topology and dimensionality changes makes the module impossible to reproduce. While Figure 4 may clarify, the textual description is insufficient on its own.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper presents AASeg, a real-time semantic segmentation network combining spatial and channel attention with multi-scale context. While the reported results (74.4% mIoU at 202.7 FPS on Cityscapes) are impressive, there are significant issues with mathematical rigor and consistency. The main results table shows mIoU around 74%, but ablation studies report ~79-80% for the same method - a major inconsistency that undermines the claims. Additionally, key equations for the attention modules are missing, and undefined operations appear in critical formulas. The cross-entropy loss description is incorrect, and notation is inconsistent throughout. These issues make it difficult to reproduce or properly evaluate the method's contributions.

comments.title: Inconsistent mIoU results between main table and ablation
comments.explanation: The main results table (Table 1) shows AASeg achieving 74.8% (val) and 74.4% (test) mIoU on Cityscapes. However, the ablation studies (Tables 4, 5, 6) show the baseline AASeg achieving 79.2% mIoU - a 4.8% absolute difference. This is a major inconsistency that undermines the paper's claims. I checked whether these might be different experimental settings, but Table 4 explicitly states it's on Cityscapes validation set, and Table 6 specifies the same 512×1024 resolution. The authors don't explain this discrepancy anywhere in the text.

comments.title: Missing equations for attention modules
comments.quote: The spatial attention (SA) module in our network is defined below: ... where W1 and W2 denotes the first and second 1 × 1 convolution layer respectively, x denotes the input data, fSigmoid denotes the sigmoid function, fReLU denotes the ReLu activation function. ... The channel attention (CA) module in our network is defined below: ... where W1 and W2 denotes the first and second 1 × 1 convolution layer, x denotes the input data. fAvgP ool[1][denotes the global average pooling function,] [ f][Sigmoid]_[ denotes the Sigmoid function,] [ f][ReLU]_ denotes ReLU activation function.
comments.explanation: The paper references equations for the spatial and channel attention modules (labeled as equations 2 and 3 in the text), but the actual mathematical formulas are missing. Only the descriptions of the components are provided. Without the actual equations, it's impossible to verify the attention mechanisms or reproduce them. This is a critical omission since attention is a core contribution of the paper.

comments.title: Undefined tensor operation in key equation
comments.quote: _xAASeg_ = (( _fSA_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ ) _⊕_ ( _fCA_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ ) _⊕_ ( _fMSC_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ )) (5)
comments.explanation: Equation 5 uses the ⊗ operation without defining it. Earlier in the paper, ⊙ is defined as convolution and ⊕ as concatenation, but ⊗ is never defined. Based on context in attention literature, ⊗ likely represents element-wise multiplication, but this is an assumption. The ambiguity makes it impossible to understand exactly how the attention weights are applied to the features.

comments.title: Incorrect cross-entropy loss description
comments.quote: where N denotes the total number of samples, yn denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is true, and 1- yn denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is false.
comments.explanation: This description doesn't match the standard cross-entropy loss formulation. The paper describes yn as a probability, but in cross-entropy for segmentation, yn should be a one-hot encoded ground truth label, and the predicted probability (typically denoted as pn or ŷn) is missing entirely. The standard formulation is L = -Σ Σ yij log(pij) where yij is 1 for the correct class and 0 otherwise, and pij is the predicted probability. The description given seems to conflate binary cross-entropy with multi-class cross-entropy.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of concatenation operator
comments.quote: We denote the concatenation operation as follows: ... where ⊕ represents the concatenation operator and x1, x2 and x3 represents the features of the two branches. ... _xfusion_ = _Upsample_ ( _xhigh_ ) _⊕_ _xlow_ (7)
comments.explanation: Equation 4 defines ⊕ as concatenation, but Equation 7 uses ⊕ to combine upsampled high-level features with low-level features. In feature fusion for segmentation, this typically involves element-wise addition or concatenation followed by convolution, not simple concatenation of tensors that may have different channel dimensions. The authors don't specify how dimension mismatches are handled, making the fusion operation ambiguous.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency for feature indices
comments.explanation: The notation _xi−_ 1 appears to be a formatting error for x_{i-1}. While this is likely an OCR issue, it creates ambiguity about whether this refers to the previous layer's output or something else. In a chain of operations, clarity about indexing is important for reproducibility.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about computational cost
comments.quote: We use Spatial Attention(SA) and Channel Attention(CA) modules to enhance features of objects without adding extra computational cost.
comments.explanation: The paper claims the attention modules add no computational cost, but attention mechanisms typically involve additional operations (global pooling, sigmoid, multiplications) that increase FLOPs. The authors don't provide any analysis or comparison to support this claim. In fact, Table 5 shows increasing GFLOPs with larger kernels, suggesting computational cost does change with architecture modifications.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel architecture, AASeg, for real-time semantic segmentation, claiming strong competitive performance on standard datasets. While the experimental results are promising, the technical documentation of the method contains significant inconsistencies, particularly regarding the mathematical definitions of the modules, internal naming of variables, and the reported performance metrics. The discrepancy between the abstract, method section, and ablation study tables leads to confusion that hinders the reproducibility and clarity of the claimed contributions.

comments.title: Inconsistent internal notation and definitions
comments.quote: xAASeg = ((fSA(xconcat) ⊗ xconcat) ⊕ (fCA(xconcat) ⊗ xconcat) ⊕ (fMSC(xconcat) ⊗ xconcat))
comments.explanation: The formula describes a combination of attention mechanisms (SA, CA) and a Multi-Scale Context module (MSC), yet the text defines SA in Eq. 2 and CA in Eq. 3, but never provides a formal definition or function for fMSC(xconcat). Furthermore, the input 'xconcat' is used here, but is not defined in the preceding subsections. This leaves the core integration logic of the proposed network underspecified.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency between text and tables
comments.quote: Our network performs better than most previous architectures with a 74.4% Mean IOU on Cityscapes test dataset while running at 202.7 FPS.
comments.explanation: While the abstract and Table 1 align on the 'AASeg' performance (74.4% mIoU, 202.7 FPS), the ablation study in Table 6 indicates that the mIoU is significantly higher (79.2% to 80.2%) for versions of the model, raising questions about whether the 'AASeg' reported in the main results is an un-optimized baseline or a different configuration entirely. The metrics in the ablation study suggest the model is capable of much higher performance than what is claimed as the final result in the table of state-of-the-art comparisons.

comments.title: Confusion in loss function variables
comments.quote: where λ1, λ2 and λ3 are set as 1, 0.5 and 0.5 to balance the loss.
comments.explanation: The text references three loss functions, but the preceding equation block defines three different loss components without clear correspondence to the λ weights. Specifically, Equation 8 is an unstructured cross-entropy, Equation 9 is an auxiliary loss, and Equation 11 is 'class attention loss'. The mathematical definitions of the weighting are missing the explicit summation formula, leading to ambiguity for a reader trying to implement the loss training regime.

comments.title: Unclear definition of convolution notation
comments.quote: We express a convolution layer Wn ( x ) as follows: where ⊙ represents the convolution operator, Wn×n represents the n × n convolutional kernel, x represents the input data and b represents the bias vector.
comments.explanation: The expression Wn(x) is introduced, but no formula follows it; the text simply lists the components. A full mathematical definition of the layer's operation (e.g., W ⊙ x + b) is missing, which is standard in such formal architecture sections. While the operation is trivial for experts, presenting it as an explicit formal definition without providing the equation is a minor documentation error.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper proposes AASeg, a real-time semantic segmentation network using spatial attention, channel attention, and multi-scale context modules. The experiments show competitive results on Cityscapes, CamVid, and ADE20K. However, the paper contains several inconsistencies, under-specified methods, and potential errors, particularly in the description of the Multi-Scale Context module, conflicting IOU numbers between the abstract and Table 1, and unclear definition of the cross-entropy loss. The ablation study also shows a discrepancy where the baseline mIoU (79.2%) does not match the main result (74.8%) for similar configurations. These issues weaken the clarity and reproducibility of the work.

comments.title: Inconsistent mIoU on Cityscapes between Abstract and Table 1
comments.quote: Our network performs better than most previous architectures with a 74.4% Mean IOU on Cityscapes test dataset while running at 202.7 FPS.
comments.explanation: The abstract states 74.4% mIoU on the Cityscapes test set. In Table 1, the row for 'AASeg' reports 'mIoU test(%)' as 74.4, which is consistent. However, the same row reports 'mIoU val(%)' as 74.8. The abstract only mentions the test set result, which is fine. But the inconsistency arises when comparing the ablation study (Table 6), where the 'AASeg-baseline' reports 79.2% mIoU on the validation set. This suggests either a different configuration or an error. The 74.8% from Table 1 vs. 79.2% in Table 6 for the validation set is a large gap that is not explained. This is confusing and undermines confidence in the results.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of Multi-Scale Context (MSC) module
comments.quote: The input feature map is convolved with dilated convolution layers with increasing dilation rates of 3, 6 and 12. The dilated convolution layer input at every stage is formed by concatenating the input feature map with the outputs from previous convolutions. At the final step, the outputs from the three dilated convolutions are concatenated with the input feature map.
comments.explanation: The description of the MSC module is vague and underspecified. It describes multiple steps: first splitting the feature map into 1x1, 3x3, and 5x5 convolutions, fusing them, then reducing channels to 256. Then it mentions using dilated convolutions with rates 3, 6, 12 on 'the input feature map'. It is unclear whether 'the input feature map' refers to the original input to the MSC module, the fused feature map, or the channel-reduced map. The phrase 'concatenating the input feature map with the outputs from previous convolutions' suggests a DenseNet-like connection within the MSC module, but the exact sequence and what 'previous convolutions' are (e.g., the 1x1/3x3/5x5 or the dilated ones) is not clear. Finally, the outputs of the three dilated convolutions are concatenated with 'the input feature map'—which input? This makes the module impossible to reproduce without guessing.

comments.title: Cross-entropy loss formula appears incorrect
comments.quote: \ell_{c e}=-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{n}^{N}\left(y_{n} \cdot \log \hat{y}_{n}+\left(1-y_{n}\right) \cdot \log \left(1-\hat{y}_{n}\right)\right)
comments.explanation: This formula is the standard binary cross-entropy loss formula, typically used for binary classification or multi-label problems. However, this paper deals with multi-class semantic segmentation (e.g., Cityscapes has 19 classes, ADE20K has 150). The text says 'cross-entropy loss function' which is fine, but the formula given is for the binary case. For multi-class segmentation, the standard is categorical cross-entropy (using softmax) or pixel-wise cross-entropy. Using binary cross-entropy per class (a multi-label approach) is possible but non-standard for single-label segmentation and is not discussed. The formula as written is mathematically incorrect for the stated multi-class segmentation problem unless the authors are deliberately using a binary formulation per channel, which is not specified. This is a clear mathematical error given the context.

comments.title: Equation numbering and reference inconsistency
comments.quote: \ell_{c e}=-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{n}^{N}\left(y_{n} \cdot \log \hat{y}_{n}+\left(1-y_{n}\right) \cdot \log \left(1-\hat{y}_{n}\right)\right) \tag{8}
comments.explanation: Equation (8) is presented but there is no 'Equation 8' in the text before this point to compare. The text references 'Equation 8' referring to this cross-entropy loss. Later, the class attention loss is defined in 'Equation 10' and 'Equation 11'. However, between Equation 8 and Equation 10, there is an auxiliary loss defined, but it is not given an equation number. The text says 'The auxiliary loss can be defined as:' followed by an unnumbered equation. This breaks the sequential equation numbering. It should be Equation 9. This is a minor typographical inconsistency but could cause confusion when referring to the loss components.

comments.title: Missing definition of terms in class attention loss
comments.quote: \ell_{c l s}=-\frac{1}{B} \sum_{i}^{B} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j}^{N} \sum_{k}^{K} I\left(g_{i j}=k\right) \cdot \log \left(a_{i j k}\right) \tag{11}
comments.explanation: The class attention loss is defined in Equation 11. The variables B, N, K, i, j, k are used as before, which is consistent. However, the symbol 'a_{ijk}' is introduced as 'the value generated of the class attention map of the j-th pixel in the i-th sample for the k-th class.' The origin of this 'class attention map' is not explained. It seems to come from the Channel Attention (CA) module, but the CA module defined in Equation 2 produces a single vector (after global average pooling), not a spatial attention map. How does this vector become a 2D map? The mechanism to generate a_{ijk} from the CA output is underspecified. A careful reader cannot compute this loss.

comments.title: Ablation study baseline does not match main result configuration
comments.quote: AASeg-baseline mIoU (%) 79.2 FPS 118.2 Parameters (M) 0.76
comments.explanation: In Table 1, AASeg at 512x1024 resolution achieves 74.8% mIoU on the validation set. In the ablation study (Table 6), the 'AASeg-baseline' achieves 79.2% mIoU on the same validation set with the same input resolution (512x1024, inferred from context). This is a 4.4% absolute difference. The 'baseline' is described as the network without some modifications, but it should serve as the reference point. If the 'AASeg' in Table 1 is the final model, it should match or be close to one of the rows in Table 6 (likely the best one, or the 'baseline' if no modifications are made). The large discrepancy suggests either a different definition of 'baseline', a different training setup (e.g., longer training, different loss weights), or an error in reporting. The paper also states 'Our network dosen't use any backbone' for AASeg, but the ablation baseline uses 0.76M parameters; it's unclear if this is the same architecture. This inconsistency is confusing.

comments.title: Ambiguous upsampling method in the main network
comments.quote: We explore the effect of 3 different upsamling operations: bilinear upsampling, deconvolution and nearest neighbor upsampling as shown in Table 4. The best results are obtained using bilinear upsampling but there isn't considerable difference.
comments.explanation: The ablation study tests three upsampling operations (Table 4). However, it is never explicitly stated which upsampling method is used in the final AASeg model reported in Table 1. The text says bilinear upsampling 'obtained the best results' in the ablation, implying it was selected, but the main results should explicitly say 'bilinear upsampling is used in our method'. Furthermore, the location of these upsampling layers in the architecture is not specified. The network description in Section 4 only mentions convolutions, attention, and concatenation. It does not describe where upsampling occurs (e.g., after the final concatenation, or between stages). This is underspecified.

comments.title: Potential sign error in auxiliary loss definition
comments.quote: \ell_{\text {aux }}=\frac{1}{B} \sum_{i}^{B} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j}^{N} \ell_{c e}\left(p_{i j}, g_{i j}\right)
comments.explanation: The auxiliary loss is defined as a positive average of cross-entropy losses. Since the cross-entropy loss itself (ℓ_ce) is already negative (from Equation 8: ℓ_ce = -1/N Σ ...), taking a positive average of a negative quantity would result in a negative loss. This is unconventional. Usually, the loss function is taken as the positive average of the negative log-likelihood (which is positive). It is likely that the authors intended ℓ_aux to be a positive scalar, but the notation is ambiguous. If ℓ_ce as defined in Equation 8 is used, the sign is wrong. The authors should clarify whether ℓ_ce in the auxiliary context is the standard cross-entropy (i.e., the negative log-likelihood) or the expression from Equation 8.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a lightweight real-time semantic segmentation network (AASeg) that combines spatial attention, channel attention, and a multi-scale context module. The problem is well-motivated, and the experimental setup across three standard datasets is appropriately comprehensive. However, the manuscript suffers from several critical gaps in mathematical specification, ambiguous architectural descriptions, and unexplained discrepancies between main results and ablation studies. While the core idea of integrating multiple attention mechanisms without a heavy backbone is valid, the missing equations, undefined operations, and inconsistent performance metrics across tables undermine the reproducibility and credibility of the claims. Addressing these technical and reporting issues is essential before the method can be properly evaluated or built upon.

comments.title: Missing mathematical formulations for core components
comments.quote: We express a convolution layer W[n] (x) as follows: [blank] where ⊙ represents the convolution operator... The spatial attention (SA) module in our network is defined below: [blank]... The channel attention (CA) module in our network is defined below: [blank]... The cross-entropy loss is calculated as defined in Equation 8: [blank]
comments.explanation: Initially, I assumed these equations were simply not rendered due to the PDF-to-OCR extraction process, which is common. However, even accounting for likely OCR artifacts, the absence of explicit formulas for the convolution block, attention modules, and loss functions leaves a critical reproducibility gap. Standard attention and loss formulations exist in the literature, but the specific dimensionalities, pooling sizes, channel reductions, and loss aggregation weights (λ1=1, λ2=0.5, λ3=0.5) are never written out. Without seeing the exact mathematical definitions, a reader cannot verify if the authors deviated from standard practices or made non-trivial design choices. This severely limits the paper's technical transparency.

comments.title: Inconsistent validation mIoU and FPS across tables
comments.quote: Table 1: AASeg mIoU val(%) = 74.8. Table 4: bilinear upsampling mIoU (%) = 79.2. Table 6: AASeg-baseline mIoU (%) = 79.2. Table 6: AASeg-baseline FPS = 118.2. Table 1: AASeg FPS = 202.7.
comments.explanation: The authors report a validation mIoU of 74.8% for AASeg in Table 1, yet the ablation studies in Tables 4 and 6 repeatedly report ~79-80% mIoU on the same validation set. Similarly, Table 1 claims 202.7 FPS, while Table 6 reports 118.2 FPS for the baseline at the same stated resolution (512×1024). While differences in evaluation protocols (e.g., single-scale vs. sliding window, crop sizes, or hardware) could explain these gaps, the authors provide no clarification. In semantic segmentation, a ~5% mIoU drop is substantial and typically corresponds to a major protocol shift. The unexplained numerical inconsistencies make it difficult to trust the reported performance ceiling or the speed-accuracy tradeoff claims.

comments.title: Ambiguous data flow in Multi Scale Context (MSC) module
comments.quote: The feature map after passing through convolutional block is split to three different parts with 1 × 1 convolution, 3 × 3 convolution and 5 × 5 convolution respectively. The individual feature maps are then fused together... The input feature map is convolved with dilated convolution layers with increasing dilation rates of 3, 6 and 12. The dilated convolution layer input at every stage is formed by concatenating the input feature map with the outputs from previous convolutions.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as a standard multi-scale parallel branch design (similar to Inception or ESPNet) followed by a dilated pyramid. However, the description contains conflicting signals: it first says the feature map is 'split' into three parts for different convolutions, which implies channel-wise partitioning. It then describes a sequential daisy-chain where each dilated convolution takes the concatenation of the original input and *previous* outputs, which is a recurrent or progressive structure. It is unclear whether the 1×1/3×3/5×5 operations are applied before or after the dilated stages, whether they are parallel or sequential, and how the 'split' relates to the 'concatenating... previous convolutions' step. Without a clear architectural diagram or explicit forward pass definition, the information flow remains ambiguous.

comments.title: Undefined notation and attention gating mechanism
comments.quote: xAASeg = (( fSA(xconcat) ⊗ xconcat ) ⊕ ( fCA(xconcat) ⊗ xconcat ) ⊕ ( fMSC(xconcat) ⊗ xconcat )) (5) where ⊕ represents the concatenation operator...
comments.explanation: Equation 5 uses the symbol ⊗ to combine attention maps with features, but it is never defined in the surrounding text. In attention literature, ⊗ conventionally denotes element-wise multiplication (Hadamard product) for feature re-weighting, but the authors do not state this, nor do they specify whether spatial dimensions are preserved, how channel mismatches are handled if any, or whether global average pooling is applied to the attention branches before weighting. Additionally, `xconcat` is only vaguely described as 'the combined feature' without specifying which earlier stage outputs are concatenated to form it. These missing conventions could lead to implementation mismatches.

comments.title: Loss function aggregation and variable mapping unclear
comments.quote: The class attention loss Lcls from channel attention module is also used... where λ1, λ2 and λ3 are set as 1, 0.5 and 0.5 to balance the loss.
comments.explanation: The text introduces three loss terms (cross-entropy, auxiliary supervision, class attention) but does not provide the final combined objective function. While the weights are listed, it is ambiguous whether λ1, λ2, λ3 correspond sequentially to `L_ce`, `L_aux`, and `L_cls`, or if there is a fourth term (e.g., total variation or L2) not mentioned. Furthermore, `L_cls` is said to come from the channel attention module, but channel attention typically outputs a 1D channel-wise weight vector, not a per-pixel class map `aijk`. The mapping between the attention mechanism's output and the per-pixel class attention loss variable `aijk` is not justified or derived, making the loss formulation logically incomplete.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper proposes AASeg, a backbone-free network for real-time semantic segmentation, incorporating Spatial Attention (SA), Channel Attention (CA), and Multi-Scale Context (MSC) modules. The work addresses a relevant efficiency problem, and the overall presentation is clean. However, the manuscript contains critical mathematical errors in the loss function definition that would prevent the model from training correctly, significant numerical inconsistencies between the ablation studies and the main results tables (specifically regarding GFLOPs and mIoU baselines), and ambiguity in the description of the MSC module's dilation strategy. These issues must be resolved before acceptance.
